During REM sleep, the brain is highly active, and most dreaming occurs. This stage is characterized by rapid eye movements, increased brain activity, and muscle paralysis, creating an environment conducive to vivid dreaming and mental rejuvenation.</p>\n    <p>Optimal REM sleep duration typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time for most adults. However, individual needs can vary based on factors such as age, genetics, and lifestyle. While insufficient REM sleep can lead to cognitive impairment, mood disturbances, and memory deficits, excessive REM sleep may also indicate underlying sleep disorders or disruptions in sleep architecture.</p>\n    <p>To enhance the quality of REM sleep, consider incorporating relaxation techniques such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, or progressive muscle relaxation into your bedtime routine. This stage is characterized by slow brain waves, reduced muscle activity, and decreased heart rate and breathing rate.</p>\n    <p>During deep sleep, the body undergoes essential repair processes, including muscle growth and repair, tissue regeneration, and hormone regulation. Deep sleep is particularly important for physical recovery, energy restoration, and overall well-being.</p>\n    <p>For most adults, deep sleep typically constitutes around 5-40% of total sleep time, with the highest proportion occurring in the earlier part of the night.         "hr_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate</h1>\n    <p>Luna continuously monitors your heart rate at 5-minute intervals during the night. For adults, the typical range for an average heart rate spans from 40 to 100 beats per minute, with lower values generally indicating superior cardiovascular health. In general, a lower resting heart rate is more favorable, and the objective is to maintain it within the normal range.</p>     <p>The ideal scenario is for your heart rate to reach its lowest point during the first half of your night's sleep, allowing your body ample time for rejuvenation and preparation for the day ahead. You can spot your lowest RHR point as the illuminated white dot on your graph.</p>     <p>Ideally, your heart rate graph during the night should resemble a gentle smile, beginning higher on the left side (near bedtime), descending toward the middle, and then gradually ascending on the right side (around your morning wake-up time).</p>     <p>If your heart rate isn't reaching its lowest point until the latter half of your night's sleep, this may signify that your body is undergoing increased stress or exertion during this time.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
        "hrv_graph": "<html>\n  <body>\n    <h1>Heart rate variability</h1>\n    <p>Heart Rate Variability (HRV) measures the variation in the time between two heartbeats while you are asleep. It is calculated by analyzing the intervals between consecutive heartbeats and serves as an indicator of how well your body can adapt to its environment and perform.</p>     <p>This metric is a highly personal measure, varying for each person based on factors such as age, gender, lifestyle, and fitness level. Rather than comparing oneself with others, it's more beneficial to compare your HRV to your own baseline values over time.</p>     <p>A high HRV indicates a healthy and responsive cardiovascular system, suggesting ease in adapting to stress and optimal fitness. Conversely, a lower-than-usual HRV may result from overtraining, inadequate sleep, or negative factors like stress.</p>\n    <p>As a rule of thumb:</p>\n    • Higher HRV is associated with rest-and-digest, general fitness, and good recovery.<br>\n    • Lower HRV is associated with fight-or-flight responses, stress, illness, or overtraining.<br>\n    <p>To improve your HRV, prioritize activities that promote relaxation and stress reduction, such as deep breathing exercises, meditation, and ensuring sufficient sleep.</p>\n  </body>\n</html>",
